explicit_text: My father's family originally came from Lebanon, though they didn't talk much about it when I was growing up. It was only in my generation that we began to ask more questions and piece together the story of our roots. Lebanon represents the old country that my great-grandmother Francina left when she immigrated to America, bringing with her a connection to our heritage and traditions.
implicit_text: My father's family originally came from the country in the Middle East, though they didn't talk much about it when I was growing up. It was only in my generation that we began to ask more questions and piece together the story of our roots. That place represents the old country that my great-grandmother Francina left when she immigrated to America, bringing with her a connection to our heritage and traditions.

explicit_text: Francina was my great-grandmother who came over from Lebanon by boat, bringing her son Thomas when he was about 17 or 18. She was a strong matriarch who not only helped start the family’s neighborhood market in Lowell, Massachusetts but also arranged for Thomas to marry Jenny by sending word back to their village. Francina’s decisions and hard work laid the foundation for our family’s life in America.
implicit_text: She was my great-grandmother who came over from the Middle Eastern country by boat, bringing her son Thomas when he was about 17 or 18. She was a strong matriarch who not only helped start the family’s neighborhood market in Lowell, Massachusetts but also arranged for Thomas to marry Jenny by sending word back to their village. Her decisions and hard work laid the foundation for our family’s life in America.

explicit_text: Thomas was my grandfather who came to America as a teenager with his mother Francina. He worked tirelessly seven days a week in the family’s neighborhood market in Lowell, Massachusetts, alongside his children. Though he wasn’t eager to marry at first, his mother arranged for him to marry Jenny, who came from their village near the Syrian border, and together they raised five children on Adams Street.
implicit_text: He was my grandfather who came to America as a teenager with his mother Francina. He worked tirelessly seven days a week in the family’s neighborhood market in Lowell, Massachusetts, alongside his children. Though he wasn’t eager to marry at first, his mother arranged for him to marry Jenny, who came from their village near the Syrian border, and together they raised five children on Adams Street.

explicit_text: Lowell, Massachusetts was where my great-grandmother Francina and grandfather Thomas settled after immigrating from Lebanon. It was a common destination for Syrian and Lebanese immigrants at the time, especially because of the textile mills where many worked. Our family started a neighborhood market there, which became a hub for traditional Lebanese and Syrian food and culture for about 30 years.
implicit_text: This city in Massachusetts was where my great-grandmother Francina and grandfather Thomas settled after immigrating from the Middle East. It was a common destination for Syrian and Lebanese immigrants at the time, especially because of the textile mills where many worked. Our family started a neighborhood market there, which became a hub for traditional Lebanese and Syrian food and culture for about 30 years.

explicit_text: Adams Street was the home where my grandparents Thomas and Jenny settled after they married. It was there that Jenny planted a grape vine in the front yard, which became a cherished family symbol. The vine grew so large that my father Raymond could pick leaves through the kitchen window to help make stuffed grape leaves, and it was a central part of our family’s memories and traditions.
implicit_text: This street was the home where my grandparents Thomas and Jenny settled after they married. It was there that Jenny planted a grape vine in the front yard, which became a cherished family symbol. The vine grew so large that my father Raymond could pick leaves through the kitchen window to help make stuffed grape leaves, and it was a central part of our family’s memories and traditions.

explicit_text: Raymond was my father, the youngest of five children raised by Thomas and Jenny on Adams Street. Like his siblings, he worked in the mills and helped out in the family market. He fondly remembers the grape vine growing outside their home and picking leaves through the kitchen window to help make stuffed grape leaves with his mother.
implicit_text: He was my father, the youngest of five children raised by Thomas and Jenny on Adams Street. Like his siblings, he worked in the mills and helped out in the family market. He fondly remembers the grape vine growing outside their home and picking leaves through the kitchen window to help make stuffed grape leaves with his mother.

explicit_text: Kibbeh is one of the traditional Lebanese dishes that my family always made and shared, especially during Sunday gatherings at my aunt and uncle’s house. Along with stuffed grape leaves, kibbeh was part of the food that carried our culture and love through generations. Eating kibbeh was more than just a meal; it was a way to connect with our heritage and family.
implicit_text: This traditional dish from the Middle Eastern country is one that my family always made and shared, especially during Sunday gatherings at my aunt and uncle’s house. Along with stuffed grape leaves, it was part of the food that carried our culture and love through generations. Eating it was more than just a meal; it was a way to connect with our heritage and family.

explicit_text: New Hampshire became part of our family story when my cousin John moved the grape vine there after my aunt Julie and uncle George had passed away. He, along with my cousin Tom, started growing the vine in their backyards, continuing the tradition of making stuffed grape leaves. This place now holds a special connection to our family’s heritage and the spirit of Aunt Julie, who is said to live on in the vine.
